title: AI Cloud Services Overview
obj: Students will be able to describe the main AI offerings of the major cloud providers and recommend an appropriate service for a given need.
content: Microsoft Azure AI
Azure AI Foundry, Cognitive Services (vision, speech, language), Azure OpenAI Service, Azure Machine Learning
AWS AI
Amazon Bedrock (foundation models), SageMaker (ML platform), Rekognition (vision), Transcribe/Polly (speech)
Google Cloud AI
Vertex AI, Gemini API, Cloud Vision/Speech/Translation, AutoML
IBM Watson
watsonx platform, Watson Assistant, Watson Discovery for enterprise NLP
How to choose
existing cloud footprint, pricing, data residency, specific model availability, compliance needs
assess: Match: given 5 use cases, recommend Azure/AWS/GCP/Watson and justify
Research: find current pricing for one service on each cloud and compare
Short answer: why would a company pick a cloud AI service over self-hosting?

title: Open-Source AI Tools and Frameworks
obj: Students will be able to identify major open-source AI tools and describe what each is used for.
content: TensorFlow
Google's deep-learning framework; production-ready; broad ecosystem
PyTorch
Meta's deep-learning framework; dominant in research; dynamic computation graph
Scikit-learn
classical ML (regression, trees, clustering); the entry point for most data scientists
Hugging Face Transformers
pre-trained NLP and vision models; one-line access to thousands of models
Spark ML
distributed ML on Apache Spark; used when data doesn't fit on one machine
License types
MIT, Apache 2.0, BSD — most AI tools are permissively licensed for commercial use
assess: Research: pick one library and find the GitHub star count, license, and major maintainers
Compare: TensorFlow vs PyTorch — 3 ways they differ
Short answer: why has the open-source ecosystem been so important for modern AI progress?

title: Proprietary AI Tools and Platforms
obj: Students will be able to compare proprietary AI platforms and understand the trade-offs vs. open-source alternatives.
content: OpenAI
GPT-4/5, DALL-E, Whisper via API; ChatGPT product; generally best-in-class
Anthropic
Claude models via API; emphasis on safety; competitive on reasoning
Google Gemini
integrated with Google Workspace; long context; multimodal
Microsoft Copilot
built on OpenAI; deep integration with Office, GitHub, Windows
Trade-offs
proprietary: top quality, easy APIs, usage fees, data policy concerns; open-source: local control, privacy, customization, requires infra
Licensing gotchas
terms of service, training-data opt-outs, commercial-use restrictions vary by provider
assess: Research: find one real company that switched from OpenAI to open-source (or vice versa) — why?
Design: for a hospital triage AI, would you pick proprietary or open-source and why?
Short answer: what concerns does sending medical data to a proprietary API raise?

title: IDEs and Packages for AI Development
obj: Students will be able to set up a Python AI dev environment and use essential packages for ML work.
content: VS Code
most popular editor; Python and Jupyter extensions; GitHub Copilot integration
PyCharm
JetBrains IDE; deep Python support; paid Pro version for scientific/data features
Jupyter Notebooks
browser-based interactive computing; standard tool for DS exploration; JupyterLab is the modern UI
Google Colab
free hosted Jupyter with GPU access; perfect for class projects
Essential packages for ML
numpy, pandas, matplotlib, scikit-learn, torch or tensorflow, transformers
Virtual environments
venv or conda; one per project; keeps dependencies clean and reproducible
assess: Lab: open Google Colab, install Pandas and Scikit-learn, load the iris dataset, print head
Compare: VS Code vs Jupyter vs Colab — when would you use each?
Short answer: why should every AI project live in its own virtual environment?

title: Agile Development and GitHub for AI Projects
obj: Students will be able to describe Agile/Scrum basics, and use Git and GitHub to version-control an AI project.
content: Agile principles
iterative, customer-focused, responds to change; short cycles (sprints) deliver working software
Scrum basics
sprint planning, daily standup, sprint review, retrospective; roles: PO, Scrum Master, dev team
Git fundamentals
commit (save snapshot), branch (parallel work), merge (combine); local history
GitHub
hosts your repo; pull requests for reviews; issues for tasks; Actions for CI
Team workflow
feature branch → push → open PR → review + CI → merge; never develop on main
AI project specifics
.gitignore for data/ and models/; Git LFS for large files; reproducibility via requirements.txt
assess: Lab: create a GitHub repo, add a README, commit 3 changes, open a pull request to yourself
Apply Agile: break a simple AI project into 3 sprints with deliverables
Short answer: why don't you commit the trained model file to Git directly?

title: End-to-End AI Project: Problem to Deployment
obj: Students will be able to walk through the full lifecycle of an AI software project from problem definition to deployment.
content: 1. Define the problem
who is the user, what decision does AI inform, what success metric, what's the business/social case
2. Collect and prepare data
source datasets, clean, split into train/val/test, document lineage
3. Model development
baseline first (simple model), iterate to more complex if baseline is insufficient
4. Evaluation
hold-out test set, compare to baseline, per-group analysis, failure case review
5. Deployment
API endpoint, containerize with Docker, monitor latency and accuracy in production
6. Monitoring and iteration
drift detection, periodic retraining, A/B testing new versions
assess: Design: walk through this 6-step lifecycle for an AI that predicts your school's cafeteria waste
Identify: given a scenario where 'accuracy dropped after deployment,' what likely went wrong?
Short answer: why must you have a baseline model before celebrating a fancy one?

title: Researching and Framing an AI Problem
obj: Students will be able to research a potential AI project, assess feasibility, and decide whether AI is the right tool.
content: User research
interview stakeholders: what do they do today, what's frustrating, what would 10x better look like
Assess AI fit
does this problem involve pattern recognition on data, or logic that's hard to code directly? if yes, AI may help
Anti-AI signal
if a simple rule works, if data is sparse, if errors are catastrophic — rethink using AI
Market research
what already exists? is there a commercial tool or open-source project you could adopt/adapt?
Feasibility check
do you have the data, compute, and skills? what's the MVP you could build in 2 weeks?
Writing a problem statement
Target user + current pain + AI intervention + success metric — all in 2-3 sentences
assess: Research: pick a problem you care about and research whether AI has been tried for it
Draft: write a 3-sentence problem statement for an AI project at your school
Short answer: name 2 problems where AI is a bad fit and explain why

title: Building a Developer Portfolio for AI Projects
obj: Students will be able to set up a public developer portfolio that showcases AI/DS projects.
content: GitHub as portfolio
pin 6 best repos, curate the profile README, keep repos well-documented
Project README essentials
problem, approach, results, how to run, screenshots, link to live demo if any
Live demos
Hugging Face Spaces (free), Streamlit Community Cloud (free), GitHub Pages for static
Personal website
short bio, project cards with thumbnails, contact info, resume link
Blog posts or dev logs
write about what you built and what you learned; Medium, Dev.to, or your own site
Signals employers look for
shipping real things, debugging breadth, clear writing, public feedback incorporation
assess: Lab: create your GitHub profile README with a bio and 3 pinned project ideas
Critique: review a professional portfolio and identify what makes it effective
Short answer: why does 'number of repos' matter less than '3 polished repos'?
